flashcards.front: Define Cache Coherence in multicore processors and explain why it is critical.
flashcards.back: Cache coherence ensures that all cores see a consistent view of memory—when one core writes to a memory location, other cores must see this updated value in their caches. Protocols like MESI (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) maintain coherence by tracking cache line states across cores.

flashcards.front: What is NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) architecture?
flashcards.back: NUMA is a memory architecture where each processor has local memory with faster access times, but can also access remote memory (attached to other processors) with higher latency. This design optimizes memory bandwidth in multicore systems but requires NUMA-aware thread scheduling.

flashcards.front: Explain SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Threads) execution model used in GPUs.
flashcards.back: SIMT executes the same instruction across multiple threads in lockstep, enabling massive parallelism. Threads are grouped into warps (32 threads on NVIDIA) or wavefronts (64 on AMD), and divergence reduces efficiency since all threads in a warp must execute the same path.

flashcards.front: What are the primary GPU memory types and their access characteristics?
flashcards.back: Global memory: large, high latency, accessible by all threads; Shared memory: low-latency on-chip, shared within a thread block; Constant memory: read-only, cached, good for uniform data; Local memory: off-chip private storage for large registers. Choosing the right memory is critical for performance.

flashcards.front: What is OpenCL and how does it enable heterogeneous computing?
flashcards.back: OpenCL (Open Computing Language) is a framework for writing programs that execute across CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, and other accelerators. It provides a C-based language (OpenCL C) and runtime API, allowing developers to write kernel functions that can be compiled and executed on any OpenCL-compatible device.

flashcards.front: Explain thread synchronization mechanisms in CUDA/OpenCL.
flashcards.back: __syncthreads() ensures all threads in a block reach a barrier before proceeding. Memory fences (__threadfence()) guarantee visibility of memory operations across threads. Atomic operations enable thread-safe counters and accumulators. Proper synchronization prevents race conditions but reduces parallelism.

flashcards.front: Define heterogeneous computing and explain the CPU-GPU execution model.
flashcards.back: Heterogeneous computing combines different processor types (CPUs + GPUs) to leverage their strengths: CPUs for sequential/control-intensive tasks, GPUs for data-parallel workloads. The host (CPU) manages memory allocation, kernel launching, and data transfer, while the device (GPU) executes parallel kernels.

flashcards.front: State Amdahl's Law and calculate speedup for a program with 90% parallelizable code running on 8 cores.
flashcards.back: Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1 / (S + P/N), where S = serial fraction, P = parallel fraction (1-S), N = number of cores. For 90% parallel (S=0.1, P=0.9, N=8): Speedup = 1/(0.1 + 0.9/8) = 1/(0.1 + 0.1125) = 1/0.2125 ≈ 4.7x. This demonstrates diminishing returns as core count increases.
